subject: Chemistry
q:
Fortification of food with iron is done using $\mathrm{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$. The mass in grams of the $\mathrm{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ required to achieve 12 ppm of iron in 150 kg of wheat is ____ (Nearest integer)
[Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{S}$ and O respectively are 56,32 and $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ]
question:
Fortification of food with iron is done using $\mathrm{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$. The mass in grams of the $\mathrm{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ required to achieve 12 ppm of iron in 150 kg of wheat is ____ (Nearest integer)
[Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{S}$ and O respectively are 56,32 and $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ]
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Let mass of iron }=\mathrm{wg} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{w}}{150 \times 10^3} \times 10^6=12 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{w}=150 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}=1.8 \mathrm{gm}
\end{aligned}$
Let mass of $\mathrm{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{w}_1 \mathrm{gm}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \text { Moles of } \mathrm{Fe}=\frac{1.8}{56}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{w}_1}{56+96+7 \times 18}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{w}_1=8.935 \mathrm{gm}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Mass of methane required to produce 22 g of CO after complete combustion is g. (Given Molar mass in g mol-1,  C=12.0,  H=1.0,  O=16.0)
question: Mass of methane required to produce 22 g of CO after complete combustion is g. (Given Molar mass in g mol-1,  C=12.0,  H=1.0,  O=16.0)
solution:
The molecular mass of methane  is 16 g/mol.

The reaction is as follows,

CH4+2O2→CO2+2H2O

Moles of CO2=massmolar mass=2244=0.5

So, required moles of CH4=0.5

 Mass of CH4 =0.5×16           =8 gm

q: A protein '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>' contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of glycine (molecular weight <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn></math>). The minimum molar mass of the protein '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>' is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> [nearest integer]

The minimum molar mass of the protein '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>' is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> [nearest integer]
solution:

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of gaseous hydrocarbon on combustion gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of water vapour. Total number of carbon and hydrogen atoms in the hydrocarbon is _______.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of gaseous hydrocarbon on combustion gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of water vapour. Total number of carbon and hydrogen atoms in the hydrocarbon is _______.
solution:
The combustion reaction can be represented as 

CxHy+x+y4O2→xCO2+yH2O

At STP, 1 mole of any gas occupies 22.4 L

10 mL of hydrocarbon, 50 mL of water and 40 mL of carbon dioxide corresponds to 0.45, 2.23,  and 1.79 moles respectively.

Hence, the combustion of 1 mole of hydrocarbon will produce 5 moles of water and 4 moles of carbon dioxide.

Thus, the molecular formula contains 4 carbon atoms and 10 hydrogen atoms.

The hydrocarbon is C4H10 and the combustion reaction is 

C4H10+6.5O2→4CO2+5H2O

x+y=4+10=14

Hence the Total number of carbon and hydrogen atoms in the hydrocarbon is 14.

subject: Chemistry
q: On complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7938</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4428</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> was produced. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> composition of oxygen in the compound is____. (Nearest Integer)
question: On complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7938</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4428</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> was produced. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> composition of oxygen in the compound is____. (Nearest Integer)
solution:
Organic compound +O2⟶  CO2g     +    H2O                                                       

                                         0.7938 gram       0.4428 gram

Weight of the organic compound=0.492 gram

Number of moles of CO2=Number of moles of C

=0.793844Weight of carbon=0.79384412=0.216g

Weight of hydrogen=0.4428182=0.0491g

Weight of oxygen=0.492-0.216-0.0491=0.2269g

% of O=0.22490.492×100=46.11≈46

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>MH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is reacted with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>MNaOH</mi></math> solution, the molarity of the resulting product <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> in the solution is____millimolar.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>MH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is reacted with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>MNaOH</mi></math> solution, the molarity of the resulting product <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> in the solution is____millimolar.
solution:
H2SO4+2NaOH→Na2SO4+2H2O

0.4mole0.2mole-0.3mol-0.1mol

Molarity=Number of molesVolume in litres

Molarity of Na2SO4 is 0.14=0.025M

=25×10-3M

Molarity of the resulting product Na2SO4 in the solution is 25 millimoles

subject: Chemistry
q: $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene on nitration gave $6.6 \mathrm{~g}$ of nitrobenzene. The theoretical yield of the nitrobenzene will be
question: $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene on nitration gave $6.6 \mathrm{~g}$ of nitrobenzene. The theoretical yield of the nitrobenzene will be
solution:
$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6+\mathrm{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ $78 \mathrm{~g} \quad 123 \mathrm{~g}$ Now since $78 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene on nitration give $=123 \mathrm{~g}$ nitrobenzene hence $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene on nitration give $=\frac{123}{78} \times 5=7.88 \mathrm{~g}$
nearest answer is (c) i.e. theoritical yield $=7.88 \mathrm{~g}$

q:
100 mL of Na3PO4 solution contains 3.45 g of sodium. The molarity of the solution is .....×10-2 mol L-1. (Nearest integer)
[Atomic Masses - Na:23.0u,O:16.0u,P:31.0u]
question:
100 mL of Na3PO4 solution contains 3.45 g of sodium. The molarity of the solution is .....×10-2 mol L-1. (Nearest integer)
[Atomic Masses - Na:23.0u,O:16.0u,P:31.0u]
solution:
Number of mole of Na+ ion =3.4523 mol

So number of mole of Na3PO4=3.453×23=0.050 mol

Molarity =0.050100×1000=0.50=50×10-2M

subject: Chemistry
q:
Molality ( \(\mathrm{m}\) ) of \(3 \mathrm{M}\) aqueous solution of \(\mathrm{NaCl}\) is :
(Given : Density of solution \(=1.25 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\), Molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: \mathrm{Na}-23, \mathrm{Cl}-35.5\))
question:
Molality ( \(\mathrm{m}\) ) of \(3 \mathrm{M}\) aqueous solution of \(\mathrm{NaCl}\) is :
(Given : Density of solution \(=1.25 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\), Molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: \mathrm{Na}-23, \mathrm{Cl}-35.5\))
solution:
3 moles are present in 1 litre solution
$\text {molality }=\frac{3 \times 1000}{1.25 \times 1000-[3 \times 58.5]}=2.79 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Chemistry
q: An oxide of iron contains $69.9\%$ iron, its empirical formula, is: (Given: Molar mass of Fe and O are $56$ and $16$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively.)
question: An oxide of iron contains $69.9\%$ iron, its empirical formula, is: (Given: Molar mass of Fe and O are $56$ and $16$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively.)
solution:
Mass percentage of $\text{Fe} = 69.9\%$

Mass percentage of $\text{O} = 100\% - 69.9\% = 30.1\%$

Number of moles of $\text{Fe} = \dfrac{69.9}{56} = 1.248$

Number of moles of $\text{O} = \dfrac{30.1}{16} = 1.881$

Molar ratio of $\text{Fe} : \text{O} = 1.248 : 1.881$

Dividing by the smallest value, the ratio is $\dfrac{1.248}{1.248} : \dfrac{1.881}{1.248} = 1 : 1.5$

Multiplying by $2$ to get the simplest whole number ratio, we get $2 : 3$.

The empirical formula is $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$.

Answer: $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$

question: 0.023×1022 molecules are present in 10gof a substance x''. The molarity of a solution containing 5g of substance 'x' in 2 L solution is _________×10−3
solution:
Number of mole ofX=6.022×10226.022×1023=10Molar mass of x

So molar mass of x=100g

Molarity =5100×2=0.025 M

Ans. 0.025 M

M=25×10-3

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of a carbonate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> on treatment with excess <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Nearest integer)
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of a carbonate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> on treatment with excess <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Nearest integer)
solution:
From Balanced chemical reaction,

M2CO3+2HCl→2MCl+H2O+CO2

Here from this, we get:

0.01 mol CO2≡0.01 mol M2CO3≡1 g M2CO3

 We know that,

Number of moles = Given massMolar mass

⇒Molar mass of M2CO3=Given mass of M2CO3Number of Moles of M2CO3=10.01=100 g/mol

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: 3.1500 g of hydrated oxalic acid dissolved in water to make 250.0 mL solution will result in 0.1 M oxalic acid solution.

Reason R: Molar mass of hydrated oxalic acid is 126 g mol–1 .

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: 3.1500 g of hydrated oxalic acid dissolved in water to make 250.0 mL solution will result in 0.1 M oxalic acid solution.

Reason R: Molar mass of hydrated oxalic acid is 126 g mol–1 .

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
Molarity (M) = wtmwt × V × 1000

Using this formula, we can calculate the molarity of the oxalic acid solution as follows:

  1. Mass of hydrated oxalic acid = 3.1500 g
  2. Molar mass of hydrated oxalic acid(H2C2O4·2H2O) = 126.064 g/mol
  3. Volume of the solution = 250.0 mL

Molarity (M)=3.15×1000126×250=0.10

So both assertion land reason are correct.

(Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Given : Atomic masses: H :1.0 u, O :16.0 u]
question:
The mole fraction of a solute in a 100 molal aqueous solution ________ ×10-2.

(Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Given : Atomic masses: H :1.0 u, O :16.0 u]
solution:
100 molal aqueous solution means there is 100 mole solute in 1 kg=1000 gm water.

Now,

mole-fraction of solute=nsolute nsolute +nsolvent 

=100100+100018=18002800=0.6428

=64.28×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q:
A 6.50 molal solution of KOHaq. has a density of 1.89 g cm-3. The molarity of the solution is mol dm-3. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Atomic masses: K:39.0 u; O:16.0 u; H:1.0 u]
question:
A 6.50 molal solution of KOHaq. has a density of 1.89 g cm-3. The molarity of the solution is mol dm-3. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Atomic masses: K:39.0 u; O:16.0 u; H:1.0 u]
solution:
6.5 molal KOH=1000 gm solvent has 6.5 moles KOH

so, wt. of solute =6.5×56

=364 gm

wt. of solution =1000+364=1364

Volume of solution =13641.89 mℓ

Molarity =mole of soluteVsolution  in Litre

=9.00

subject: Chemistry
q:
The elemental composition of a compound is $54.2 \% \mathrm{C}, 9.2 \% \mathrm{H}$ and $36.6 \% \mathrm{O}$.
If the molar mass of the compound is $132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the molecular formula of the compound is :
[Given : The relative atomic mass of $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{H}: \mathrm{O}=12: 1: 16$ ]
question:
The elemental composition of a compound is $54.2 \% \mathrm{C}, 9.2 \% \mathrm{H}$ and $36.6 \% \mathrm{O}$.
If the molar mass of the compound is $132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the molecular formula of the compound is :
[Given : The relative atomic mass of $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{H}: \mathrm{O}=12: 1: 16$ ]
solution: Molecular mass of compound $=132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ Molecular formula of compound is $\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{O}\right)_n$ $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\mathrm{EF} \text { mass }}=\frac{132}{44}=3$ $\therefore$ Molecular formula of compound is $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
$2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is left after removing $10^{21}$ molecules from its ' $x$ ' mg sample. The mass of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ taken initially is
Given: $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.02 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
question:
$2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is left after removing $10^{21}$ molecules from its ' $x$ ' mg sample. The mass of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ taken initially is
Given: $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.02 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
solution:
Moles of removed $\mathrm{CO}_2=\frac{10^{21}}{6.02 \times 10^{23}} \mathrm{~mol}$
$=1.66 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$
mole of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ left $=2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{moles}$ total moles of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ taken initially
$=(2.8+1.66) \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$
mass of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ taken initially
$\begin{aligned}
& =4.46 \times 10^{-3} \times 44 \\
& =196.24 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g} \\
& =196.24 \mathrm{mg}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The density of a solution prepared by dissolving $120 \mathrm{~g}$ of urea (mol. Mass $=60 \mathrm{u}$ ) in $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $1.15 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molarity of this solution is :
question: The density of a solution prepared by dissolving $120 \mathrm{~g}$ of urea (mol. Mass $=60 \mathrm{u}$ ) in $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $1.15 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molarity of this solution is :
solution:
Total weight of solution $=1000+120=1120 \mathrm{~g}$
$\text { Molarity }=\frac{120}{60} \times \frac{1000}{1120 / 1.15}=2.05 \mathrm{~M}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of mass percentage (w/w) of C : H in a hydrocarbon is $12 : 1$. It has two carbon atoms. The weight (in g) of $CO_2(g)$ formed when $3.38$ g of this hydrocarbon is completely burnt in oxygen is : (Given : Molar mass in g mol$^{-1}$ C : 12, H : 1, O : 16)
question: The ratio of mass percentage (w/w) of C : H in a hydrocarbon is $12 : 1$. It has two carbon atoms. The weight (in g) of $CO_2(g)$ formed when $3.38$ g of this hydrocarbon is completely burnt in oxygen is : (Given : Molar mass in g mol$^{-1}$ C : 12, H : 1, O : 16)
solution:
Ratio of mass percentage of C : H = $12 : 1$

Ratio of moles of C : H = $\dfrac{12}{12} : \dfrac{1}{1} = 1 : 1$

The empirical formula is $CH$. Since the hydrocarbon has two carbon atoms, its molecular formula is $C_2H_2$.

Molar mass of $C_2H_2 = 2 \times 12 + 2 \times 1 = 26$ g mol$^{-1}$

Moles of $C_2H_2$ in $3.38$ g = $\dfrac{3.38}{26} = 0.13$ mol

The combustion reaction is:

$C_2H_2 + \dfrac{5}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + H_2O$

From the stoichiometry, $1$ mole of $C_2H_2$ produces $2$ moles of $CO_2$.

Moles of $CO_2$ produced = $2 \times 0.13 = 0.26$ mol

Molar mass of $CO_2 = 12 + 2 \times 16 = 44$ g mol$^{-1}$

Weight of $CO_2$ formed = $0.26 \times 44 = 11.44$ g

Answer: $11.44$

[Given : Atomic masses : C=12.0 u, H:1.0 u, O:16.0 u, Br=80.0 u]
question:

[Given : Atomic masses : C=12.0 u, H:1.0 u, O:16.0 u, Br=80.0 u]

= moles of m-bromobenzoic acid

So, weight of m-bromobenzoic acid

=6.1122×201 gm

=10.05 gm

% yield =Actual weightTheoretical weight×100

=7.810.05×100

=77.61%

subject: Chemistry
q:
116 g of a substance upon dissociation reaction, yields 7.5 g of hydrogen, 60 g of oxygen and 48.5 g of carbon. Given that the atomic masses of H,O and C are 1,16 and 12, respectively. The data agrees with how many formulae of the following?

A. CH3COOH

B. HCHO

C. CH3OOCH3

D. CH3CHO
question:
116 g of a substance upon dissociation reaction, yields 7.5 g of hydrogen, 60 g of oxygen and 48.5 g of carbon. Given that the atomic masses of H,O and C are 1,16 and 12, respectively. The data agrees with how many formulae of the following?

A. CH3COOH

B. HCHO

C. CH3OOCH3

D. CH3CHO
solution:
Organic Compound containg C,H,O⟶dissociationH7.5gram+O60gram+C48.5gram% of H in the given organic compound =7.5116×100=6.465%

(A) % of H=460×100=6.6%

(B) % of H=230×100=6.6%

(c) % of H=662×100=7.677%

(D) % of H=444×100=9.09%

The percentage of Hydrogen in the given organic compound is equal to 6.465% which is approximately equal to the percentage of hydrogen in CH3COOH and HCHO.

What would be the maximum uptake of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> ions by the resin if expressed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mol</mi></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>gm</mi></math>?
solution:
2C8H7SO3Na aq+Ca2+ aq→C8H7SO32Ca aq+2Na+aq

1 mol calcium ion reacts with 2 moles of resin

2 mol resin=412 gresin≡40 gCa2+ions
=1 mol of Ca2+ions
∴ Moles of Ca2+ per g of resin=1412

subject: Chemistry
q: A $5.2$ molal aqueous solution of methyl alcohol, $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$, is supplied. What is the mole fraction of methyl alcohol in the solution?
question: A $5.2$ molal aqueous solution of methyl alcohol, $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$, is supplied. What is the mole fraction of methyl alcohol in the solution?
solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Molefraction of solute }\left(\mathrm{X}_2\right) \text { in aqueous solution } & =\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}+\frac{1000}{18}} \\ & =\frac{5.2}{5.2+\frac{1000}{18}}=0.09\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: How many grams of methyl alcohol should be added to $10$ litre tank of water to prevent its freezing at $268 \mathrm{~K}$ ? ( $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for water is $1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg}$ $\left.\mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$
question: How many grams of methyl alcohol should be added to $10$ litre tank of water to prevent its freezing at $268 \mathrm{~K}$ ? ( $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for water is $1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg}$ $\left.\mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_f=\mathrm{K}_f m$
where $m=$ molality
$273-268=1.86 \times \frac{w}{M \times V}$
$5=1.86 \times \frac{w}{32 \times 10}$
$w=\frac{5 \times 32 \times 10}{1.86}$
$=860.2 \approx 868.06 \mathrm{~g}$
